entities.label: University of Michigan CME digital text Merlin
notes: The current XML identifies the source as Wheatley's edition and retains a call number ending in 1969.
The current XML omits printed front matter and the Old French transcription on printed pages 700-701; it includes Wheatley's English terminal continuation on pages 697-699.
The header says editorial notes were not included, but 49 NOTE1 elements remain. That statement should be read as selective or imprecise rather than as a literal zero-note claim.

bibliographic.citation: Merlin; or, The Early History of King Arthur: A Prose Romance (about 1450-1460 A.D.), ed. Henry B. Wheatley, 4 parts, Early English Text Society, Original Series 10, 21, 36, 112 (London: Trubner; Kegan Paul, Trench, Trubner & Co., 1865-1899).
bibliographic.pages: Middle English and supplied English text pp. 1-699; Old French source transcription pp. 700-701; index pp. 703-749; glossary pp. 751-776
bibliographic.publisher: Trubner & Co.; later Kegan Paul, Trench, Trubner & Co.
bibliographic.series: Early English Text Society, Original Series 10, 21, 36, 112
bibliographic.title: Merlin; or, The Early History of King Arthur: A Prose Romance (about 1450-1460 A.D.)
bibliographic.volume: 4 parts; later bound and titled in 2 volumes

dateStatements.label: serial publication and completed issue
dateStatements.value: Parts published 1865, 1866, 1869, and 1898/1899; completed two-volume title issue 1899

entities.label: Henry B. Wheatley's EETS edition of Merlin
notes: Wheatley imposed chapter divisions on a manuscript organized by paragraphs rather than chapters.

The Cambridge witness breaks off on printed page 697. Wheatley's English continuation through page 699 is translated from BL Add MS 10292; the following Old French source transcription on pages 700-701 is not present in the repository XML.
At printed pages 387-392 Wheatley translated text from Add MS 10292 to fill damage in Cambridge ff. 137-138; note 48 uses the French manuscript for one phrase at printed page 650.

rights.basis: The edition and completed issue were published no later than 1899 and are outside the U.S. copyright term.
rights.component: Wheatley's nineteenth-century text, notes, translation, and editorial matter
notes: This assessment does not override provider conditions on particular scan files.

entities.description: The principal and only continuous Middle English witness of the Prose Merlin, nearly complete but damaged and ending imperfectly.

entities.label: Cambridge University Library MS Ff.3.11

physicalDescription.extent: 245 extant leaves from an original 248; Prose Merlin at ff. 1r-245v, ending imperfectly
notes: Pink records ff. 246-248 as lost and ff. 137-138 as damaged by burning.
Wheatley's older advertisement instead says two leaves were missing at the end.

physicalDescription.extent: Guardbook of 129 leaves; Romance of Merlin at fol. 43r-v
physicalDescription.format: Composite collection of fragments bound together circa 1861

rights.basis: The fragment is late medieval and outside modern copyright terms.

entities.description: A richly illuminated 1316 Old French Lancelot-Grail volume containing L'estoire del Saint Graal and L'estoire de Merlin. Wheatley used narrowly identified passages of its Merlin text to repair, compare, and continue his Middle English edition.
entities.label: British Library Add MS 10292
notes: This record does not assert that Add MS 10292 was the physical exemplar used by the anonymous Middle English translator.

physicalDescription.dimensions: 400 x 295 mm; text space 295 x 240 mm
physicalDescription.extent: 216 folios plus four unfoliated paper flyleaves; L'estoire de Merlin at ff. 76r-216v
physicalDescription.format: Three columns of 50 lines; Gothic script; 233 miniatures
notes: Origin: northern France, Saint-Omer or Tournai.
The Wheatley supplement loci are only a tiny part of the complete French Merlin section.

rights.basis: The manuscript text dates from 1316 and is far outside modern copyright terms.
rights.jurisdiction: United States and United Kingdom term assessment
notes: Modern photographs, catalog text, and reproduction-service deliverables are separate components.

next_steps: Seek U-M digitization-production notes or a catalog record tied to call number 820.6 E13 no.10, 112 1969.
notes: The current evidence makes Greenwood probable but does not identify a barcode or copy-specific source.

next_steps: Compare Wheatley's advertisements across issues with current CUL collation records.
If possible, inspect the final quire physically or through commissioned images.
openQuestions.question: Why does Wheatley's 1865 statement say two final leaves are missing while Pink and current METS report three?

next_steps: Submit a Cambridge imaging request if the user chooses to seek a whole-codex facsimile.
Record any quotation, conservation feasibility decision, permitted uses, and exact deliverable when received.
openQuestions.question: Can Cambridge supply or expose a complete color facsimile of MS Ff.3.11, and under what price and publication terms?

next_steps: Consult specialist textual studies comparing the Middle English version with the Vulgate Merlin manuscript tradition.
Do not promote Add MS 10292 from Wheatley's editorial support source to translator exemplar without evidence.
openQuestions.question: Which physical Old French Vulgate Merlin exemplar, if any surviving manuscript, lay behind the anonymous Middle English translation?

next_steps: Recheck the current BL catalogue and digitized-manuscripts services.
Meanwhile use the BL request route or IRHT MF 28722.
openQuestions.question: Will the British Library restore public full digital coverage for Add MS 10292?

next_steps: Treat each statement as applying to its documented item and version.
Seek written U-M clarification before relying on the current statement to override legacy contractual language for the pinned file.

relations.subjectLabel: John Conlee's METS edition of Prose Merlin

reviewNotes: The anonymous Middle English work, the physical witnesses, Wheatley's edition, U-M's encoding, and the repository XML are separate layers.

The current U-M XML and the pinned repository XML preserve the same broad printed-page scope and structure but are not byte-identical. The current file restores richer CHOICE/CORR/SIC markup at loci where the pinned transformation flattened or rearranged elements.
The U-M source header lists EETS numbers 10 and 112, but U-M's own collection list and external catalogs identify the complete four-part edition as Original Series 10, 21, 36, and 112.
The U-M call number ends in 1969, supporting but not conclusively proving the Greenwood reprint as the keyed physical carrier. The relation is therefore supported rather than confirmed.

Rawlinson D. 913 is a 129-leaf nineteenth-century guardbook. Its Merlin fragment occupies physical fol. 43r-v; the Digital Bodleian object exposes only fol. 43r of that work and only ten selected images from the guardbook overall.
British Library Add MS 10292 is modeled only as passage-scoped French support used by Wheatley. No evidence establishes it as the anonymous Middle English translator's exemplar.

Pink and the current METS introduction say three final Cambridge leaves are lost, whereas Wheatley's 1865 advertisement says two. Both assertions are preserved as an unresolved historical-description conflict.
Local cache completeness describes the downloaded provider object, not completeness of the physical codex or of the work.

sourceLinks.entityLabel: John Conlee's METS edition of Prose Merlin
sourceLinks.format: HTML reading edition and downloadable PDF

rights.basis: METS states that current digital editions are for individual use and that reprinting the digital text requires contacting the Executive Director; XML is separately licensed CC BY-NC-SA 4.0.
notes: No METS XML file was downloaded or modeled as a local copy here; the XML license must not be generalized to all display text or PDF output.

lineage.summary: The repository artifact is a pinned 1997 University of Michigan CME XML representation of Henry B. Wheatley's four-part EETS edition of the Middle English Prose Merlin. The U-M header and call number indicate that the immediate physical carrier was probably the 1969 Greenwood photo-reprint, while Wheatley's text was printed in four parts from 1865 through the completed 1899 issue. Cambridge University Library MS Ff.3.11 is the principal nearly complete Middle English witness; Bodleian MS Rawl. D. 913, fol. 43r-v preserves a second short fragment. Wheatley supplied three narrowly bounded kinds of material from the Old French Vulgate Merlin in British Library Add MS 10292: text lost through burning at Cambridge ff. 137-138, one phrase at a blank, and the terminal continuation after the Cambridge manuscript breaks off. The last continuation is Wheatley's modern English translation, not text transmitted by the Middle English witness. Public scans, partial manuscript images, request routes, and reuse terms are recorded per entity rather than treated as one undifferentiated source.

catalogSummary.author: Anonymous Middle English translator and adapter; first source section ultimately associated with Robert de Boron
catalogSummary.date: Near the middle of the fifteenth century; c. 1450-1460 is the conventional range printed by Wheatley
catalogSummary.region: England; exact place and dialect localization unresolved
catalogSummary.title: Middle English Prose Merlin

catalogingSubject.label: Middle English Prose Merlin
catalogingSubject.scope_note: The anonymous mid-fifteenth-century Middle English prose translation and adaptation conventionally called the Prose Merlin. It is one intellectual work with two source-compositional sections, not thirty-three independent works. The two surviving Middle English witnesses, the Old French Vulgate Merlin and its manuscripts, Henry B. Wheatley's edition and modern English supplements, the U-M digital encoding, and the repository XML are distinct transmission or representation layers. In particular, the repository item's final printed pages 697-699 include a Wheatley translation from Old French after the principal Middle English manuscript breaks off; that terminal editorial supplement is described for representation accuracy but is not silently attributed to the anonymous Middle English translator.

contentParts.label: Robert de Boron source section

locators.value: first five METS sections, through Arthur's coronation

notes: The six Wheatley chapters and five METS sections are different editorial segmentations of the same analytical source section.
No explicit medieval division marker was established at the transition.
contentParts.summary: From the council of devils and Merlin's birth through Vortigern, Pendragon, Uther, Arthur's birth, the sword in the stone, and Arthur's coronation. This section is probably derived ultimately from Robert de Boron's late-twelfth-century verse Merlin through the prose/Vulgate tradition.

contentParts.label: Vulgate Merlin sequel section

locators.value: DIV1.7 through the CUL break inside DIV1.33; printed pp. 108-697; manuscript-folio milestones approximately 36a-245b
from after Arthur's coronation through the break at f. 245v/b

notes: Wheatley's translations at printed pp. 387-392 and p. 650 repair selected local defects within this section; they do not turn the whole section into a modern translation.
The final Wheatley continuation after the manuscript break is modeled separately below.
contentParts.summary: The long interlaced sequel after Arthur's coronation: rebellion and Saxon war, Arthur's alliances with Ban and Bohort, campaigns in Britain and Gaul, Gawain and other young knights, Arthur and Gonnore, Roman conflict, Merlin and Nimiane, Merlin's imprisonment, and the final episodes transmitted by the Cambridge witness.

contentParts.label: Wheatley's terminal English supplement

locators.value: inside DIV1.33 after note.49; remainder of printed p. 697 through p. 699

notes: This is present in the repository representation but excluded from medieval author/translator scope.
The terminal supplement's extent is described by print pagination because the English text does not occur in a Middle English manuscript witness.
contentParts.summary: A modern English continuation supplied after CUL Ff.3.11 breaks off, translated by Wheatley from the Old French Vulgate Merlin in BL Add MS 10292. It completes the printed and encoded reading text through p. 699 and is followed in print by the Old French source transcription on pp. 700-701, which U-M omitted.

dateStatements.display: Near the middle of the fifteenth century; conventionally c. 1450-1460
notes: Wheatley's title prints about 1450-1460, which supplies the normalized interval; current METS more cautiously says near the middle of the fifteenth century. The interval is a conventional scholarly estimate, not an exact documentary date.

dateStatements.display: Fifteenth-century attestation in CUL MS Ff.3.11 and late-fifteenth-century attestation in Rawlinson MS D. 913; chronological priority is not established
notes: The broad date ranges overlap, and the checked sources do not establish which surviving witness is chronologically first.
Witness copying dates are not automatically the exact translation date.

dateStatements.display: First part published in 1865; completed EETS edition issued by 1899
notes: The edition appeared in four parts; 1865 records the first publication of the work in print, not completion of the whole edition.

extent.description: One prose work represented in 33 Wheatley-created chapter DIV1 elements. The pinned XML has 34 HEAD elements including the body heading, 603 P elements, 699 PB elements for printed pages 1-699, 490 manuscript-folio MILESTONE elements, and 49 NOTE1 elements. The 33 chapters are representation metrics, not thirty-three intrinsic works; the two source-compositional sections are analytical, and Wheatley's terminal translation is separately modeled.

formStatements.description: A sustained prose Arthurian romance. The Old French verse Merlin stands behind the first source-compositional section, but the cataloged Middle English work is prose throughout.

genres.label: Translation and adaptation
notes: The work closely follows the Old French Vulgate Merlin; this genre statement records textual mode rather than implying known word-for-word dependence on a surviving exemplar.

languageStatements.label: Middle English
notes: Modern English and Old French in Wheatley's edition are separately scoped editorial/source layers.

scope.description: The medieval English work through the surviving Middle English witness text

languageStatements.label: English
notes: The classification is linguistic/editorial, not a claim that the medieval work as a whole is mixed Middle and Modern English.

scope.description: Wheatley's English translations at printed pp. 387-392, p. 650, and pp. 697-699
Brief French comparison readings retained in Wheatley's encoded notes; the full French transcription on print pp. 700-701 is omitted

notes: The 33 XML chapters are Wheatley's editorial organization. Conlee's current METS edition uses 25 sections. Neither number is promoted to an intrinsic medieval-work item count.
The Cambridge witness is the only continuous Middle English copy, not the only surviving Middle English material: Rawl. D. 913 preserves a short independent fragment.
The analytical two-section model concerns source composition. The third content part in this manifest is a repository-representation supplement introduced by Wheatley after manuscript loss.
BL Add MS 10292 is evidence for the Old French source tradition and for Wheatley's explicit supplements; it is not asserted to be the Middle English translator's physical exemplar.
Dates, places, languages, rights, and access claims belong to their documented layers and should not be flattened across work, witness, edition, encoding, and facsimile.

next_steps: Review specialist linguistic and textual scholarship for attribution proposals, preserving disputed or hypothetical names as such.
openQuestions.question: Can the anonymous Middle English translator or adapter be identified more narrowly?

next_steps: Consult LALME/eLALME and specialist studies of the Cambridge and Rawlinson language.
Keep authorial and scribal dialect claims separate.
openQuestions.question: What place of translation and dialect region can be supported for the Middle English work, distinct from the scribal localization of either witness?

next_steps: Review codicological, paleographical, and linguistic dating studies.
Do not equate witness copying date with composition date without argument.
openQuestions.question: Can the conventional c. 1450-1460 dating be narrowed or independently justified beyond Wheatley's title and the broad current description 'near the middle of the fifteenth century'?

next_steps: Consult specialist textual comparisons across the Vulgate Merlin tradition.
Treat BL Add MS 10292 only as Wheatley's passage-scoped editorial source until direct translator-exemplar evidence is found.
openQuestions.question: Which Old French Vulgate Merlin manuscript or textual family supplied the Middle English translator's exemplar?

next_steps: Compare Conlee's first-five-section boundary, Wheatley's chapter VI ending at printed p. 107, and the corresponding manuscript folio sequence in a full facsimile.
openQuestions.question: Can the Robert de Boron/Vulgate-sequel transition be located more precisely in every witness and edition without treating later section or chapter counts as intrinsic?

next_steps: Reconcile Wheatley's two-leaf statement with Pink's and current METS's three-leaf collation using current CUL codicological evidence.
openQuestions.question: Did the principal Cambridge codex lose two or three leaves at the end?

notes: The reviewed sources place the Middle English work in England but do not securely localize an exact place of translation.

scope.description: Principal legendary setting, including Logres, Wales, Cornwall, Camelot, and conflicts with Saxons and rebel kings
Major campaigns and episodes involving Ban, Bohort, Claudas, Trebes, and Roman conflict

notes: No personal name for the Middle English translator is securely established in the reviewed sources.

scope.description: Middle English rendering of the Old French Vulgate Merlin, excluding Wheatley's modern terminal supplement in the repository representation

notes: Conlee calls the work a straightforward and fairly accurate translation; 'adapter' preserves the modern cataloging description without implying a radically independent rewriting.

scope.description: Selection, wording, and adaptation involved in producing the Middle English prose version

notes: The Middle English section is probably derived through a prose/Vulgate transmission rather than proved to translate Robert's surviving verse directly.

scope.description: Authorship of the late-twelfth-century Old French verse source ultimately underlying the first section

responsibilities.agentName: Anonymous Vulgate Merlin continuator or continuators
notes: This source-layer responsibility does not displace the anonymous Middle English translator.

scope.description: Authorship of the Old French prose sequel source incorporated in the Vulgate Merlin

notes: This is edition-level responsibility and is not medieval work authorship.

scope.description: Transcription, chaptering, notes, and nineteenth-century EETS presentation underlying the U-M encoding
Modern English continuation after CUL Ff.3.11 breaks off, translated from BL Add MS 10292 and encoded at printed pp. 697-699

notes: The repairs occur at printed pp. 387-392 and p. 650; they are not a whole-part replacement.

scope.description: English repairs translated from BL Add MS 10292 for text lost through burning at CUL ff. 137-138, and one phrase supplied at a blank

subjects.label: Merlin
notes: The subject coverage includes Merlin's birth, prophecy, enchantment, and imprisonment.

subjects.label: The birth, coronation, and rise of King Arthur
Chivalry, tournaments, warfare, and military alliance
Magic, prophecy, disguise, and supernatural knowledge
The Round Table and formation of Arthurian fellowship
Preparation for the Holy Grail tradition

summaries.value: The Middle English Prose Merlin recounts Merlin's demonic conception and Christian preservation, his prophetic childhood, the fall of Vortigern, the reigns of Pendragon and Uther, Arthur's conception and coronation, and the long military and dynastic consolidation of Arthur's realm. Its Vulgate-sequel section expands into interlaced campaigns, courtly alliances, tournaments, prophecies, the formation of Arthurian fellowship, Merlin's dealings with Nimiane, and the events surrounding his imprisonment.

The work is a close Middle English prose translation/adaptation of the Old French Vulgate Merlin. Scholars distinguish an opening section ultimately associated with Robert de Boron's verse Merlin, ending with Arthur's coronation, from an anonymous early-thirteenth-century Vulgate sequel. The division is analytical and source-historical: the Middle English text merges the sections without a marked internal break.

The repository XML follows Wheatley's 33 editorial chapters and printed pages 1-699. It includes modern English text supplied by Wheatley from BL Add MS 10292 at damage/blank loci and after the Cambridge witness breaks off, but omits Wheatley's printed Old French transcription on pages 700-701. Those features belong to the edition/encoding representation and are not silently assigned to the anonymous medieval translator.

notes: The qualifier 'Middle English' distinguishes the work from Robert de Boron's poem, the Old French Vulgate Merlin, other Merlin continuations, and modern editions.
This common short title is human-readable but can be ambiguous outside a Middle English context.

scope.description: Title of Wheatley's source edition and the U-M item rather than a securely transmitted medieval title

titles.value: Merlin; or, The Early History of King Arthur: A Prose Romance

scope.description: Editorial heading at the beginning of Wheatley's text and the U-M XML

notes: Because the principal Middle English manuscript breaks off earlier, this encoded trailer is not treated as evidence for the medieval work's original ending.

scope.description: Wheatley-edition trailer after the French-derived terminal continuation

titles.value: Explicit the shutting up of Merlin. May God bring us all to a good end!

work.summary: The Middle English Prose Merlin recounts Merlin's demonic conception and Christian preservation, his prophetic childhood, the fall of Vortigern, the reigns of Pendragon and Uther, Arthur's conception and coronation, and the long military and dynastic consolidation of Arthur's realm. Its Vulgate-sequel section expands into interlaced campaigns, courtly alliances, tournaments, prophecies, the formation of Arthurian fellowship, Merlin's dealings with Nimiane, and the events surrounding his imprisonment.
